html:
Salomon August Andrée, often referred to as S. A. Andrée, was born on October 18, 1854, in Gränna, and died in October 1897 on White Island (northeast of Spitsbergen). He was a Swedish patent engineer, physicist, and polar explorer, best known for the Andrée Polar Expedition, an unsuccessful attempt in 1897 to reach the North Pole using a hydrogen balloon.

== Biography == Andrée was the son of pharmacist Claes Georg Andrée and Jacobina (Mina) Gustafva, née Heurlin (daughter of pastor Elias Heurlin), who died on April 29, 1897. He attended Jönköping's higher secondary school from 1863 to 1870, and was a regular student at the Technological Institute from 1871 to 1874. Andrée worked as a draftsman at Göranssons Mechanical Workshop in Stockholm from 1874 to 1876, took a study trip to England and America in 1876, served as foreman at Norrahammar Ironworks and Spånhult Mechanical Workshop from 1877 to 1878, was a partner in the firm Andrée & Möller and worked at Stillefors Ironworks from 1878 to 1879. He was an assistant in physics at the Royal Institute of Technology from 1880 to 1882, a chief engineer at the Patent Office from 1884 to 1897, and a senior lecturer in experimental physics at the Technical School in Stockholm from 1886 to 1889. Andrée served on the Stockholm City Council from 1891 to 1894 and was a member of the Swedish Technological Society from 1872 to 1897. He was also an advocate for free trade and equality between men and women.

== The Andrée Polar Expedition ==

=== Balloon Flights and the Attempt from Spitsbergen ===

Andrée became known in Sweden for making the first balloon journey from Sweden to Finland. However, he had very limited experience – only 40 hours – of balloon flying before he published his plans for a balloon expedition to the North Pole. From Gothenburg, Andrée made several balloon flights, for example, on February 26, 1894, at noon, when he launched from the new gasworks near Gullbergsvass and landed three kilometers from the railway station in Vara, and on August 4th, also from the same starting point, to Trollhättan, in two hours. His ideas generated enthusiasm among the public, and he received financial support primarily from Alfred Nobel, Oscar II, and Oscar Dickson.

The first attempt to carry out an expedition to the North Pole was made in June 1896 with the balloon "Örnen," along with Nils Ekholm and photographer Nils Strindberg. The members of the expedition departed from Gothenburg harbor from the New Quay on the steamship "Virgo," heading for Spitsbergen and the northwest coast of Danskøen, and embarked at Pikes House in "Virgo's" harbor on June 22, 1896, where the equipment was unloaded. From their location, at 79 degrees 43 minutes north latitude, they began to erect the balloon hangar with the help of the crew of the "Virgo," in very poor weather conditions. The balloon was ready and filled for departure for three weeks, between July 27th and August 17th, but the few winds blowing from the south were too weak to be used for any significant journey northwards. Therefore, on August 17th, they decided to pack up the balloon and the technical equipment and return to Gothenburg, as there was too great a risk in attempting a journey further north. The land beyond 82 degrees latitude north was also unexplored. The balloon hangar, securely anchored against the wind, along with half a tank of hydrogen, was left behind for use in future expeditions. On Saturday, August 29, 1896, the "Virgo," captained by Zachau, could be moored at the New Quay in Gothenburg harbor.

Ekholm withdrew from the project in October 1896, believing it to be too risky. In particular, the meteorology of the Arctic Ocean – especially the winds – was insufficiently understood. He was replaced as a crew member for the next attempt by engineer Knut Frænkel. Gustaf Wilhelm Emanuel Svedenborg was also appointed as a substitute in case any of the participants were unable to participate.

=== The Örnen Expedition === On July 11, 1897, with favorable southerly winds, Andrée and his companions Fraenkel and Strindberg ascended in the hydrogen balloon "Örnen" towards the North Pole. Almost immediately after takeoff, problems arose when parts of the mooring lines (which were invented by Andrée to steer and control the speed of the balloon, as well as to add some weight for the planned altitude) detached at a joint lower down. However, Andrée was able to repair this using spare ropes he had brought along. Additionally, the five mooring lines were slightly misplaced, causing the balloon to rotate halfway after takeoff. After just over two days, the air journey ended (the valves were opened) because humid air created ice that weighed down the balloon. They then began their trek eastward across the ice towards the depot on Franz Josef Land. However, the ice carried them westward, so they changed course towards Svalbard and the depot on Sjuöya in northern Svalbard. For three months, the three men struggled on the ice, which was drifting further south, before setting up camp on Vitön where the ice floe met land. There, they died for reasons that have not been definitively established: first Strindberg, then Andrée and Fraenkel.

=== Legacy === Except for a carrier pigeon and some recovered buoys, no news of the expedition was received for 33 years. The remains of the expedition members, along with their tools and diaries, were discovered in 1930 on Vitön. This was a media event of enormous proportions. The diaries had been preserved so well that their contents could still be read.

How certain are we about this?

People from the 19th century are very well documented (census records, newspapers, letters). The uncertainty mainly concerns selection and perspective – whose stories are highlighted.
tldr: S.A. Andrée was a Swedish inventor and researcher who became famous for his failed attempt to reach the North Pole by balloon in 1897. He and his two companions perished on the ice, and their diaries were not discovered until 33 years later.
lattlast:
S.A. Andrée was a trained engineer and physicist who worked on various projects before becoming famous for his attempts at flight. He held positions as a teacher and worked at the Patent Office, the agency responsible for protecting new inventions. He received support from prominent figures like Alfred Nobel to attempt reaching the North Pole using a hydrogen-powered balloon.

Before the major expedition, he undertook several smaller test flights within Sweden. In 1897, he embarked on the ambitious journey with two other men. The flight did not go as planned because ice formed on the balloon, forcing them to land. They drifted on the ice for three months before reaching an island called Vitön, where all three ultimately perished.

The fate of the expedition remained unknown until 1930, when their diaries and equipment were discovered on the island. Because the diaries were well-preserved, it was possible to read about their experiences during their time lost. The story of their journey became major news when the artifacts were found.
